html:
Seigakusha is not the net-cash software story in the prior draft. It is better understood as a tiny, ignored Japanese education operator that has shifted from shrinking class-based cram schools into individual tutoring, online tutoring, childcare, and specialized schools while the market still values it like a no-growth local juku.

At ¥790 as of May 15, 2026, the stock is on only 7.2x company-guided FY3/27 EPS of about ¥110, 1.01x book, and a 4.18% dividend yield, before the 100-share QUO card benefit that matters to Japanese retail holders. FY3/26 was not stale trailing noise: sales rose 6.1% to ¥15.16B, operating profit rose 25.8% to ¥979M, EPS rose to ¥105, and management guided FY3/27 to sales of ¥16.22B, operating profit of ¥1.06B, net income of ¥609M, and a dividend increase from ¥22 to ¥33. Source: FY3/26 results, Yahoo quote.

The variant perception is simple: this is being priced as a demographic melting ice cube, but the live numbers show mix shift plus pricing power. Individual tutoring enrollment grew 4.3% year over year at the November peak, while class instruction fell 5.8%. Management kept total direct classrooms flat in FY3/26, closing weaker locations while opening in Tokyo, Kanagawa, Chiba, and Saitama. For FY3/27, they plan 13 new direct classrooms, 5 new franchise classrooms, peak enrollment of 29,134 students, up 4.0%, and a continued Kanto expansion push. This is not a liquidation. It is a slow regional roll-up of higher-touch tutoring capacity.

The market is missing the operating leverage. FY3/26 revenue came in basically in line with the old plan, but operating profit beat by 18.4% and net income beat by 35.9% because personnel, materials, and recruitment advertising costs came in below forecast. That matters because the bearish view assumes wage inflation and student acquisition costs will consume all growth. Instead, the company showed that even modest enrollment growth and tuition revisions can drop through when the class-school footprint is rationalized and the individual tutoring mix rises. Source: forecast versus actual release.

The business is more complex than the ticker screen suggests. Seigakusha runs Free Step individual tutoring, Kaisei Education Seminar class instruction, Link One online tutoring, childcare centers, Japanese language schools for foreign students, and a small digital content and e-learning subsidiary, Naspia. The core is not a pure software platform, but the company does own enough workflow and learning-management infrastructure, including My Step Log and S-CUBE, to make the tutoring model more scalable than old classroom teaching. Source: company business overview.

The balance sheet is not the giant net-cash bargain described in the prior thesis. Cash and deposits were ¥2.58B at March 2026, but borrowings and leases were roughly ¥2.27B, so net cash is only about ¥0.3B. Still, the stock is cheap on the right metric: roughly ¥4.1B EV against FY3/27 operating profit guidance of ¥1.06B, or about 3.8x forward EV/EBIT. Add depreciation around ¥400M and EV/EBITDA is below 3x. Free cash flow after capex was about ¥640M in FY3/26, a mid-teens yield on the treasury-adjusted equity value.

Retail context is also favorable in a neglected way. The Yahoo board is nearly silent, with discussion centered on the QUO card, dividends, and whether the stock can hold ¥800, not on a serious earnings inflection. Yahoo’s sentiment poll had effectively no participation. That is useful: the stock has not been discovered, promoted, or crowded. Source: Yahoo board.

The clean thesis is that Seigakusha deserves to move from “1x book, 7x earnings, dead juku” to “profitable, 14% ROE education services compounder with a 30% payout and Kanto expansion.” A 10 to 12x forward P/E is not aggressive for a company growing sales mid-single digits, guiding operating profit to a record, and paying a 4% cash yield. The dividend policy is also improving: FY3/27 payout is guided at 30%, and the company still offers the ¥1,000 QUO card for 100-share holders. Source: shareholder return policy.

The main risks are not subtle. Japan’s child population is shrinking, class instruction is declining, labor costs can rise faster than tuition, and management may over-expand in Kanto. Governance is also imperfect: control is concentrated through related Ota-family entities, and the company has lease and shareholder relationships that outside investors cannot easily influence. The stock is illiquid, with only 6,800 shares traded on May 15. Position sizing has to reflect that.

But the asymmetry is still attractive because expectations are low, the current earnings base is not heroic, and the shareholder return now pays investors to wait.

Bear target: ¥550, down 30%. Class instruction decline accelerates, Kanto openings disappoint, margins fall back toward 4%, and the market values the stock at 0.7x book or 5x to 6x depressed earnings.

Base target: ¥1,300, up 65%. FY3/27 guidance is achieved, EPS is about ¥110, the dividend holds at ¥33, and the stock rerates to roughly 12x forward earnings, still modest for a 14% ROE company.

Bull target: ¥2,100, up 166%. Kanto expansion works, individual tutoring and online offerings keep growing, operating profit approaches ¥1.5B over the next few years, EPS moves toward ¥155 to ¥165, and the stock earns a 13x multiple as the market stops treating it like a melting ice cube.

Current stock price: ¥790. Base case upside: about 65%. Bull case upside: about 166%.
